description: Airborne particles are the most widespread cause of chronic, year-round, or seasonal allergic rhinitis (commonly known as hay fever).

allergens.name: Pollen
allergens.description: Fine powder released by trees, weeds, and grasses that spikes seasonally.

allergens.name: Dust Mites
allergens.description: Microscopic bugs that thrive in household dust, bedding, and carpets, causing year-round symptoms.

allergens.name: Animal Dander
allergens.description: Tiny flakes of skin, fur, feathers, or saliva shed by domestic pets like cats and dogs.

allergens.name: Mold Spores
allergens.description: Airborne fungi spores that grow in damp, humid indoor or outdoor environments.

description: While you can be allergic to almost any food, nine specific foods account for roughly 90% of all severe food allergy reactions: [1, 2, 3]

allergens.name: Peanuts
allergens.description: Legumes grown underground that frequently trigger severe, lifelong reactions.

allergens.name: Tree Nuts
allergens.description: Tree-grown nuts including almonds, walnuts, cashews, hazelnuts, and pistachios.

allergens.name: Cow’s Milk
allergens.description: One of the most common childhood allergies, though many children outgrow it.

allergens.name: Eggs
allergens.description: Another highly prevalent childhood allergen, primarily triggered by proteins in the egg white.

allergens.name: Shellfish
allergens.description: Divided into crustaceans (like shrimp, crab, and lobster) and mollusks (clams, oysters), which often cause severe adult-onset reactions.

allergens.name: Fish
allergens.description: Finned fish such as salmon, tuna, and cod.

allergens.name: Soy
allergens.description: Common in processed foods, though often outgrown by adulthood.

allergens.name: Wheat
allergens.description: Distinct from celiac disease, this is an immune response to wheat proteins.

allergens.name: Sesame
allergens.description: Recognized globally as a priority allergen due to increasing prevalence and risk of severe reactions.

allergens.name: Mustards
allergens.description: Mustard is a priority food allergen. It is an immune system response that can cause severe, life-threatening anaphylaxis.

allergens.name: Sulphites
allergens.description: Sulphites are preservatives, not a true allergy. They cause a non-allergic sensitivity (intolerance) that most commonly triggers asthma symptoms, wheezing, or skin irritation.

allergens.name: Medications & Drug Allergies
allergens.description: Certain medications can cause the immune system to overreact, resulting in hives, rashes, or respiratory distress.

allergens.name: Penicillin
allergens.description: Penicillin and related antibiotic variants are the most common sources of drug allergies.

allergens.name: NSAID
allergens.description: Over-the-counter pain relievers such as aspirin and ibuprofen.

allergens.name: Insect Stings
allergens.description: Venom injected during an insect bite or sting can spark localized swelling or full-body anaphylaxis.

allergens.name: Stinging Insects
allergens.description: Honeybees, hornets, wasps, yellowjackets, and fire ants.

allergens.name: Skin & Contact Allergens (Contact Dermatitis)
allergens.description: Direct skin contact with specific materials or chemicals can induce localized rashes, itching, and blisters.

allergens.name: Latex
allergens.description: Natural rubber latex found in medical gloves, elastic bands, and balloons.

allergens.name: Fragrances and Preservatives
allergens.description: Chemicals used in cosmetics, soaps, and laundry detergents.

allergens.name: Nickel
allergens.description: A common metal used in jewelry, belt buckles, and zippers.

allergens.name: Poison Ivy/Oak
allergens.description: Plants containing urushiol oil, which triggers an intensely itchy rash upon touch.
